title: VA Disability Claims | Second Amendment Rights | Survivors Benefits
summary:
For the first time since 2020, VA’s disability claims backlog has dropped below 100,000. That’s a 63% reduction since January 2025, when the backlog stood at more than 264,000. 
 
A claim is considered backlogged after 125 days. Today, only 17% of claims hit that mark — compared to 70-percent back in 2013. VA processed more than three million disability and pension claims in fiscal year 2025 -- an all-time record. 
— 
VA is moving to restore Second Amendment rights to Veterans in its Fiduciary Program. 
 
For decades, Veterans who needed help were automatically reported to the FBI’s background check system as prohibited from owning firearms — without a hearing, and without any legal basis. 
 
VA has ended that practice and is working with the FBI to remove past reports from the system, so no Veteran loses their constitutional rights. 
— 
If you’re a surviving family member of a Veteran, VA is making it faster to get your benefits. 
 
Starting February 23rd, VA will no longer require separate decisions on Dependency and Indemnity Compensation and Survivors Pension. Instead, VA will pay whichever benefit is higher. 
 
Questions? Call the VA benefits hotline at 1-800-827-1000. 
 
— 
 
Explore VA.gov to learn about your benefits. 
 
Print out your VA Welcome Kit: https://www.va.gov/welcome-kit/ 
 
Whether you’re just getting out of the service or you’ve been a civilian for years now, the VA Welcome Kit can help guide you to the benefits and services you’ve earned. Based on where you are in life, your VA benefits and services can support you in different ways. 
 
Sign up for the VetResources email newsletter: https://news.va.gov/vetresources/ 
 
VetResources features Veteran-centric news stories, various deals and discounts, employment opportunities, and much more. 
 
#Veterans #VA #VABenefits #Healthcare

title: Operation Epic Fury: Why Trump’s Iran Strikes Don’t Need Congress
summary:
Operation Epic Fury: Why Trump’s Iran Strikes Don’t Need Congress

Is it inconsistent for Democrats to label President Trump a dictator over “Operation Epic Fury” against Iran while ignoring U.S. history?

Have presidents of both parties not led multiple undeclared wars and military actions, and does Article II not grant the president authority to initiate and direct military operations?

Is the 1973 War Powers Act unnecessary and unconstitutional, especially in light of Nancy Pelosi’s statement that President Obama did not need congressional authorization to bomb Libya?

Has the United States not effectively been at war with Iran since 1979, with Iran and its proxies killing at least 1,000 Americans?

Does waiting not risk a catastrophic nuclear attack, and did President Trump not act to protect Americans and U.S. allies, including Israel?

title: It’s Anyone’s Race in the Governor’s Primary
summary: No top candidates for governor have emerged in June’s primary race, as California’s likely voters are dividing their support across two Republicans and three Democrats. The top issue is clearer: Affordability.

title: Aging Strong with VA: Adult Day Health Care
summary:
In this episode of Aging Strong with VA, , Deputy Secretary Paul R. Lawrence, PhD, discusses Adult Day Health Care. He breaks down how Adult Day Health Care provides daytime health services, supervision, and meaningful social interaction for Veterans living at home. It also supports caregivers by offering reliable care during the day while keeping Veterans safe and engaged.

Watch the episode below.

In Part 2 of our Glad You Asked series, Deputy Secretary Paul R. Lawrence, Ph.D. talks to Margarita Devlin and they take a deeper look at what happens after you apply for Veteran Readiness & Employment (VR&E). 

They breakdown how entitlement is evaluated, what you can expect during your initial meeting with a counselor, and how career planning helps identify what is needed for you to accomplish your goals.

title: Have Racial Disparities in Law Enforcement Stops Narrowed?
summary: California’s Racial and Identity Profiling Act (RIPA) requires detailed reporting on all pedestrian and traffic stops. Recent RIPA data from the state’s largest law enforcement agencies points to a reduction in the overall number of stops—but we do not see a substantial narrowing of racial/ethnic disparities in intrusive experiences during stops.

source: Publication Archive - Public Policy Institute of California
title: Policy Brief: Have Racial Disparities in Law Enforcement Stops Narrowed?
summary: Recent data from the state’s largest law enforcement agencies points to a reduction in the overall number of stops, especially by police departments. There is limited evidence of a narrowing of racial/ethnic disparities in intrusive experiences during stops.

ARLINGTON, Va. ― Concerned Veterans for America (CVA) is closely monitoring reports of U.S. military strikes against targets in Iran. While details remain limited and the full scope of the operation is still emerging, any direct military engagement with Iran carries significant risks for American service members and for U.S. interests across the Middle East […]

title: An Ecuadorian Marine’s Path to the U.S. Marine Corps
summary: Long before earning the title of United States Marine, Lance Cpl. Juan G. Ipialesparedes served as a special operations Marine in his native country of Ecuador. Today, as a personnel administration specialist with 1st Marine Logistics Group, I Marine Expeditionary Force, he brings a lifetime of military experience and a deep respect for service that transcends nationality to the United States.

title: Statewide Survey: Californians and Their Government
summary: Ahead of the primary elections, Californians share their views of the top issues facing the state and nation. Our latest survey examines support for candidates in the gubernatorial primary race and opinions of Governor Newsom’s proposal to address the state budget deficit. The survey also explores views of federal cuts to Medicaid and sentiment toward ICE. Survey analyst Lauren Mora will outline key findings and discuss takeaways.

title: Water Quality in California
summary: Californians deserve a safe water supply, but the state is plagued by a complex mixture of historic, ongoing, and emerging threats to water quality. Our updated fact sheet highlights the progress underway—and details measures that might help.

source: The Official United States Marine Corps Public Website News
title: Beneath Norway's Mountains
summary: Above the Arctic Circle, winter dominates the landscape. Snow blankets the mountains and subzero winds cut across frozen valleys. For most people, this terrain can feel remote and inhospitable. However, for U.S. Marines alongside NATO Allies and partners, it is a proving ground. In the Arctic, the environment can be as lethal as any adversary. This reality makes joint readiness, training, and the ability to rapidly mobilize equipment and personnel to crisis not optional, but essential to operational success in the event of armed conflict.

title: California’s Businesses
summary: Most California businesses are small, and most Californians are employed by small businesses. Well-established businesses are responsible for most jobs, but job growth is stronger among new businesses. Business ownership does not mirror California’s demographic makeup.
